items.q: Does validation prove that entered data is correct?
items.correct: No - validation only checks data is sensible and allowed (right type, range or format); a wrong birthday can still pass if it is a valid date.
items.misconception: Yes - data that passes validation must be accurate and true.
items.random_wrong: Yes - validation compares every entry with the real world.
items.why: Reasonable is confused with true. Validation tests rules, not reality - which is why verification methods like double entry exist alongside it.

items.q: Can a primary key value appear in more than one record of its table?
items.correct: No - the whole point of a primary key is uniqueness; duplicate key values would make records impossible to identify reliably.
items.misconception: Yes - duplicates are fine if the other fields are different.
items.random_wrong: Yes - primary keys repeat once the table has many records.
items.why: Students see two different people called Smith and relax the rule. That is exactly why artificial IDs are used as keys instead of names.

items.q: Are a field and a record the same thing?
items.correct: No - a field is one column (a single category of data), while a record is one row (all the data about one entity instance).
items.misconception: Yes - field and record are interchangeable database words.
items.random_wrong: A field is a row and a record is a column.
items.why: The grid layout invites swapping rows and columns. Anchor it: Surname is a field; everything about one particular student is a record.

items.correct: A field in one table that refers to the primary key of another table
items.misconception: The first field of every table
items.why: Correct answer: A field in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. “The first field of every table” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A range check is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Validation. “Encryption” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a database table, a record is:
items.correct: One row holding all the data about one entity instance
items.why: The answer examiners want is: One row holding all the data about one entity instance. “One column of the table” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The purpose of a primary key is to:
items.correct: Uniquely identify each record in the table
items.why: It's Uniquely identify each record in the table. “Sort the table alphabetically” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A table has 7 fields and 30 records. How many individual data values does it hold?
items.why: Exact answer: 210 values. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
